> *Your designated savings covers about 2.2 months of current spending.*

## Recommended target

You should hold **$16,200 as your minimum emergency fund**, then build toward **$32,400** for stronger protection.

| Figure | Amount |
|---|---:|
| Observed monthly spending | **$5,400** |
| 3-month minimum | **$16,200** |
| 6-month target | **$32,400** |
| Current savings | **$12,000** |
| Gap to minimum | **$4,200** |

**Recommended:** Direct your observed **$900 monthly surplus** to savings until it reaches $16,200. That should take about **five months**. Afterward, continue toward $32,400 if income is variable, either earner’s job is uncertain, or your family relies heavily on childcare.

You also have **$18,500 in checking**, so your total bank cash is $30,500—nearly the six-month target. If at least $4,200 of checking is genuinely surplus rather than money reserved for upcoming bills, you may already satisfy the minimum.

**Data note:** This uses the April–June 2026 complete-month average, as of July 13. I can’t identify rent or mortgage costs fully, so the target may need adjustment once housing is confirmed. Your brokerage was excluded because emergency reserves should remain stable and immediately accessible.
